brief_document.title: Implement TypeScript conversations, backpressure, connection, and codegen
brief_document.purpose: Complete the TypeScript SDK with Conversation<T> lifecycle management (ADR-001), backpressure as discriminated unions (ADR-004), connection lifecycle and reconnection, and a type generator that produces TypeScript interfaces from liminal channel definitions. After this brief, TypeScript developers get IDE autocompletion and type checking for channel messages without writing type definitions manually (story S8).
brief_document.task: Create conversation.ts, pressure.ts, connection.ts, and codegen.ts in sdks/liminal-ts/src/. Conversation<T> mirrors the Rust ConversationHandle with lifecycle events as a TypeScript-idiomatic interface. PressureResponse is a discriminated union matching the Rust enum. The connection module implements the same state machine as SDK-003 (connecting, connected, reconnecting, disconnected) with exponential backoff. The codegen module reads liminal channel type definitions and generates TypeScript interfaces. IN SCOPE: conversations, backpressure unions, connection lifecycle, type generation. OUT OF SCOPE: channel logic (SDK-007), WASM bridge changes (SDK-007).

requirements.title: Define Conversation<T> interface with lifecycle events
requirements.spec: WHEN a TypeScript developer opens a Conversation<T>, THE SYSTEM SHALL return a handle with send, receive, and close methods. THE SYSTEM SHALL emit lifecycle events (opened, message, closing, closed, error) as an observable stream. WHEN the conversation errors, THE SYSTEM SHALL emit an error event with a typed reason before transitioning to closed. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT silently drop lifecycle transitions.
acceptance: Conversation<T> interface has send(message: T): Promise<void> method
Conversation<T> interface has receive(): AsyncIterable<T> method
Conversation<T> interface has close(): Promise<void> method
Conversation<T>.lifecycle returns an AsyncIterable<ConversationEvent> or EventEmitter
ConversationEvent is a discriminated union with kind: 'opened' | 'message' | 'closing' | 'closed' | 'error'
Error events carry a typed reason field

requirements.title: Define backpressure discriminated unions
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a PressureResponse discriminated union with three variants: { kind: 'accept' }, { kind: 'defer', delay: number } (delay in milliseconds), and { kind: 'reject', reason: string }. WHEN Channel.publish returns a PressureResponse, THE SYSTEM SHALL preserve the variant without flattening or converting. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT silently convert 'defer' or 'reject' to 'accept'.
acceptance: PressureResponse is a discriminated union type with kind as the discriminant
Accept variant: { kind: 'accept' }
Defer variant: { kind: 'defer', delay: number } where delay is in milliseconds
Reject variant: { kind: 'reject', reason: string }
Channel.publish return type is Promise<PressureResponse>
TypeScript narrowing works: if (response.kind === 'defer') { response.delay } compiles

requirements.title: Implement connection lifecycle and reconnection
requirements.spec: WHEN the SDK connects to an liminal server, THE SYSTEM SHALL manage the connection through states: connecting, connected, reconnecting, disconnected. WHEN the connection drops, THE SYSTEM SHALL automatically reconnect using exponential backoff with jitter. WHEN reconnection succeeds, THE SYSTEM SHALL recover subscriptions from their last acknowledged sequence. THE SYSTEM SHALL emit connection state change events for observability. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT use fixed-interval retry.
acceptance: ConnectionState is a discriminated union: 'connecting' | 'connected' | 'reconnecting' | 'disconnected'
Connection emits state change events observable via onStateChange callback or EventEmitter
Reconnection delay follows exponential backoff: min(baseDelay * 2^attempt, maxDelay) + jitter
After reconnection, subscriptions resume from their last acknowledged sequence
No fixed-interval retry code path exists
baseDelay and maxDelay are configurable via ConnectionConfig

requirements.title: Implement type generator from channel definitions
requirements.spec: WHEN a developer runs the type generator against liminal channel type definitions, THE SYSTEM SHALL produce TypeScript interface files matching each channel's message schema. THE SYSTEM SHALL generate interfaces with proper field names, types, and JSDoc comments. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT require manual type definition for channels that have liminal-registered schemas.
acceptance: codegen.ts exports a generate function that accepts channel type definitions and outputs TypeScript source
Generated interfaces match channel message schemas with correct field names and TypeScript types
Generated files include JSDoc comments with the source channel name and schema version
A channel with fields { name: string, count: integer } produces interface { name: string; count: number; }
The generator is usable as a CLI tool or programmatic API

boundaries: SHALL NOT duplicate channel publish/subscribe logic from SDK-007.
SHALL NOT modify the WASM bridge from SDK-007.
SHALL NOT silently consume backpressure signals.
SHALL NOT use fixed-interval retry for reconnection.
verification: TypeScript compilation (tsc --noEmit) succeeds with no errors
Conversation lifecycle events emit in correct order: opened -> message* -> closing -> closed
PressureResponse discriminated union narrows correctly in TypeScript
Connection reconnection uses exponential backoff verified by unit test
Type generator produces valid TypeScript from a test channel definition
Generated TypeScript interfaces compile without errors
No file exceeds 500 lines of code

adrs.title: Conversations as the fundamental messaging unit, not messages
adrs.decision: The fundamental unit is the conversation — a supervised beamr process owning a structured exchange between parties — over the message. A conversation mediates lifecycle, failure handling, and (optionally) durability. Exchange patterns (request-reply, streaming, saga) are conversation types with different lifecycle rules, not special-case bolt-ons. The rejected alternative is the NATS/Kafka model: messages as the primitive, patterns assembled from messages.

adrs.title: Backpressure as a wire protocol primitive with accept/defer/reject signals
adrs.decision: Backpressure is part of the wire protocol with three signals: accept (delivered), defer (buffered, will deliver when capacity frees), reject (consumer overwhelmed, message shed). Consumers declare capacity. The bus mediates between producer rate and consumer capacity. The rejected alternative is Kafka-style consumer-lag-only backpressure (not actionable by producers in real time).

checklist.text: Type generator produces TypeScript interfaces from liminal channel type definitions.

stories.text: As a TypeScript developer, I want generated TypeScript interfaces for channel message types so that I get IDE autocompletion and type checking without writing type definitions manually.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
The Rust SDK must compile with no_std + alloc for WASM targets.
Backpressure responses must never be silently consumed by the SDK. Every pressure signal reaches the application.
Reconnection attempts use exponential backoff with jitter. No fixed-interval retry loops.
The TypeScript SDK must work in both browser (WASM) and server (Node.js) environments without conditional imports in application code.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, developers in three ecosystems can connect to liminal and use its full feature set — channels, conversations, typed messages, backpressure — with APIs that feel native to each language. The Rust SDK is the primary interface for embedded use: Aion workflows, co-located services, and any Rust application that wants zero-hop messaging. The Gleam SDK is the authoring convenience layer: message types defined in Gleam get compile-time enforcement, and conversation handlers are authored as Gleam modules running in beamr. The TypeScript SDK serves browser WASM and server-side Node.js, bringing liminal to web applications and the JavaScript ecosystem.

The experience should be: import the SDK, connect (or embed), and start conversing. Connection management, reconnection, subscription state recovery, and backpressure handling are SDK responsibilities — application code deals with messages and conversations, not transport.
